critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression experiment for disease susceptibility prediction using genomic data has several fundamental methodological problems.

1. LABEL CONSTRUCTION IS CIRCULARLY DEFINED AND WILL PRODUCE AN EASY CLASSIFICATION PROBLEM. Labels are assigned: positive if family history AND >=3 known GWAS SNPs; negative if no family history AND <2 GWAS SNPs. Known GWAS SNPs are exactly the features the transformer is supposed to learn from the SNP genotype data. If known disease-associated SNPs from GWAS are both used to construct the label and included as features (since the full ~500K SNP set includes these SNPs), then the label is directly decodable from the features — the model does not need to learn any nonlinear interaction, it simply needs to count the known SNPs. This makes the comparison between transformer and logistic regression trivial: logistic regression can detect this linear signal perfectly, and any transformer advantage will not reflect the model's ability to learn novel interactions.

2. PREPROCESSING FIT ON COMBINED DATASET IS EXPLICIT DATA LEAKAGE. Section 3 states: 'SNP filtering...MAF < 5% in the combined data set' and 'Normalization: z-score normalization...with mean and std computed from the combined dataset.' The combined dataset includes test samples. MAF filtering using test sample allele frequencies means the feature space is informed by test-set population structure. If the test set contains rare variants specific to certain ancestry groups, MAF filtering using combined data may retain or exclude features based on test-set composition.

3. POPULATION STRATIFICATION CONFOUND IS ONLY PARTIALLY ADDRESSED. The stratified random split ensures each ancestry group is proportionally represented across splits. However, within each ancestry group, there may be fine-scale population structure (e.g., European samples span Northern, Southern, Eastern European sub-populations) that is not stratified. Fine-scale structure can create spurious associations between ancestry-correlated SNPs and the disease label if the label prevalence differs by subpopulation. The design acknowledges 'population stratification as a known major confounder' but does not include principal components as covariates in the models, which is standard practice in genomic association studies.

4. TRANSFORMER ON 50,000-100,000 SPARSE SNP FEATURES WITH N=200,000 IS MISSPECIFIED. A transformer with 2-4 blocks and 8 attention heads on 50,000-100,000 SNP features requires a tokenization or embedding strategy that is not described. Transformer attention over raw SNP vectors is computationally intractable at this dimensionality without dimensionality reduction or chunking. The design does not specify how SNPs are tokenized or grouped. If SNPs are processed as individual tokens with full self-attention, the quadratic attention complexity on 50,000 tokens is computationally infeasible. This suggests the transformer architecture as described cannot be implemented as specified.

5. AMBIGUOUS-LABEL EXCLUSION CREATES SPECTRUM BIAS. Labels are assigned only to clear positive (family history AND >=3 SNPs) and clear negative (no family history AND <2 SNPs) cases, with the middle range excluded. This artificially sharpens the discrimination task by removing the ambiguous cases that are hardest to classify and most representative of real clinical uncertainty. Performance on the easy-split population will substantially overestimate performance on the full-spectrum population including excluded cases.
all_issues_raised: Label construction uses known GWAS SNPs that are also present as features; the model can decode the label by counting known SNPs without learning novel interactions
MAF filtering and normalization computed on combined dataset including test samples constitutes data leakage through feature space selection
Fine-scale population structure within ancestry groups is not controlled; principal components are not included as covariates, standard practice in genomic studies
Transformer self-attention on 50,000-100,000 SNP tokens is computationally infeasible as specified; tokenization strategy is absent
Excluding ambiguous-label cases creates spectrum bias; performance on the easy-split population overestimates real-world performance
all_issues_adjudicated: Label construction uses known GWAS SNPs that are also present as features; the model can decode the label by counting known SNPs without learning novel interactions
MAF filtering and normalization computed on combined dataset including test samples constitutes data leakage through feature space selection
Fine-scale population structure within ancestry groups is not controlled; principal components are not included as covariates, standard practice in genomic studies
Transformer self-attention on 50,000-100,000 SNP tokens is computationally infeasible as specified; tokenization strategy is absent
Excluding ambiguous-label cases creates spectrum bias; performance on the easy-split population overestimates real-world performance

critic_raw:
This transformer-based sentiment analysis experiment for heart failure readmission prediction has multiple serious methodological flaws.

1. PREPROCESSING FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING IS EXPLICIT DATA LEAKAGE. Section 3 states: 'preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits.' This is a critical error. The TF-IDF vocabulary for the baseline is fit on test-period discharge summaries. The numerical scaling for clinical covariates is informed by test-set patient characteristics. The result is that the baseline TF-IDF representation is constructed with knowledge of test-period language patterns, and both models receive scaled clinical features informed by the test distribution.

2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON TEMPORAL CLINICAL DATA IS INCORRECT. The design uses stratified random sampling without enforcing temporal ordering. Patient admissions from 2023 can appear in training while 2019 admissions appear in the test set. Heart failure management evolved substantially over this window (COVID-19 impact, SGLT2 inhibitor adoption). Random temporal mixing produces evaluation estimates that do not reflect prospective deployment. This is compounded by the validation set being defined as 'Months 21-26' and test set as 'Months 27-36' in Section 6, which contradicts the random sampling approach in Section 2.

3. PRIMARY METRIC IS MAE ON A CONTINUOUS RISK SCORE THAT IS NOT DEFINED. Section 5 states the primary metric is MAE on a 'readmission risk score.' But the outcome in Section 1 is defined as binary (30-day readmission yes/no). A continuous risk score is described as something that 'will also be modeled if available,' implying it is a secondary or optional target. The primary metric (MAE) is applied to a target that may not exist in the data. If the binary label is the actual outcome, AUC-ROC is the appropriate primary metric, and MAE is inapplicable. The experimental design conflates a regression primary metric with a classification task.

4. CLINICIAN SENTIMENT ANNOTATIONS AS FEATURES ARE METHODOLOGICALLY PROBLEMATIC. Clinician sentiment annotations on a 'validated scale' are included as features for readmission prediction. Clinicians who annotate sentiment are likely aware of the patient's subsequent course — including readmission. If annotations are made retrospectively (as is common in EHR-based research), they may encode implicit knowledge of outcome, creating direct outcome leakage through the sentiment feature. The design does not specify whether annotations are prospective or retrospective, which is critical.

5. TRANSFORMER MODEL TRAINS ON FROZEN BERT EMBEDDINGS WITH NO FINE-TUNING. Section 4 describes a shallow feed-forward network trained on pre-extracted BERT embeddings. The BERT model itself is not fine-tuned on readmission prediction. This means the text representation captures general biomedical language semantics but not readmission-specific patterns. The TF-IDF baseline, by contrast, builds a vocabulary specifically optimized for this dataset's text. Comparing frozen pretrained BERT embeddings to task-specific TF-IDF is not a fair evaluation of transformer vs. bag-of-words methods — it tests whether general pretraining beats task-specific feature engineering, which is a different question.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing including TF-IDF vocabulary and numerical scaling fit on entire dataset before splitting is explicit data leakage for both models
Stratified random split contradicts the temporal validation/test window definitions in Section 6; split strategy is internally inconsistent
Primary metric MAE requires a continuous risk score that is not a defined outcome; binary label makes MAE inapplicable as primary metric
Clinician sentiment annotations may be retrospective and encode outcome information, creating direct label leakage through a feature
Frozen pretrained BERT embeddings vs. task-specific TF-IDF compares general pretraining vs. task-specific feature engineering, not transformer vs. bag-of-words architectures
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing including TF-IDF vocabulary and numerical scaling fit on entire dataset before splitting is explicit data leakage for both models
Stratified random split contradicts the temporal validation/test window definitions in Section 6; split strategy is internally inconsistent
Primary metric MAE requires a continuous risk score that is not a defined outcome; binary label makes MAE inapplicable as primary metric
Clinician sentiment annotations may be retrospective and encode outcome information, creating direct label leakage through a feature
Frozen pretrained BERT embeddings vs. task-specific TF-IDF compares general pretraining vs. task-specific feature engineering, not transformer vs. bag-of-words architectures

critic_raw:
This TCN vs. linear regression experiment for ED wait time prediction is one of the better-structured designs in this set, with a genuine temporal split, but it retains several methodological issues.

1. PREPROCESSING FIT ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING IS DATA LEAKAGE. Section 1 states: 'missing staffing levels and bed counts (~3% of records) are imputed using median values computed on the entire dataset.' Section 2 further states: 'All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' Imputing from test-period data and normalizing by test-period statistics are methodological errors even for a design that otherwise has a correct temporal split. The rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals computed on the full dataset means test-period arrival patterns inform the contextual feature for training records.

2. TCN RECEIVES SEQUENTIAL CONTEXT THAT LINEAR REGRESSION DOES NOT. The TCN architecture takes 12-timestep input sequences (12-hour arrival history). The linear regression baseline receives only the scalar value of the rolling 6-hour mean. This is a feature representation asymmetry: the TCN has access to the full temporal trajectory of the 12 preceding hours, while linear regression has a single aggregate. Any performance advantage of the TCN over linear regression is attributable to richer temporal feature access, not to TCN's architectural capability for sequence modeling. To fairly test TCN's architectural contribution, the linear regression should receive all 12 timesteps flattened as features.

3. EXCLUDING PATIENTS WITHOUT PRIOR VISITS IN LAST 12 MONTHS CREATES CASE MIX BIAS. The design uses only encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months. First-visit patients and patients with gaps longer than 12 months are excluded. In an ED setting, new patients (no prior visits) may have systematically longer or shorter wait times due to triage complexity, insurance status, or acuity distribution. The model trained on repeat visitors will not generalize to first-time presentations, which may constitute a substantial fraction of ED volume. This selection criterion is not discussed as a limitation.

4. PAIRED T-TEST ON ABSOLUTE ERRORS WITHIN A TEST SET HAS TEMPORAL AUTOCORRELATION. The design proposes a paired t-test on per-encounter absolute errors to test statistical significance. ED encounters within the same time window have correlated wait times due to shared staffing conditions and patient census. The i.i.d. assumption underlying the paired t-test is violated. Autocorrelated error pairs inflate the degrees of freedom, producing p-values that are too small. A block bootstrap or mixed-effects model respecting temporal clustering would be appropriate.

5. WAIT TIME AS A TARGET IS PARTIALLY WITHIN THE MODEL'S CONTROL IN DEPLOYMENT. The model predicts time-to-clinician-contact as a fixed environmental variable. In deployment, if predictions are used by ED managers to allocate staff, the actual wait times change in response to the predictions. The evaluation assumes wait times are stationary outcomes, but operational use of the model creates feedback effects. This is not a critique of the experimental evaluation per se, but the design does not acknowledge that the deployment context makes the prediction target endogenous to model use.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting (imputation from test-period medians, StandardScaler on combined data) is data leakage despite correct temporal split
TCN receives 12-timestep sequences while linear regression receives a scalar aggregate; performance difference reflects feature richness, not architectural capability
Excluding patients without prior 12-month visits creates case mix bias; model will not generalize to first-time ED presentations
Paired t-test on autocorrelated ED encounter errors violates i.i.d. assumption; temporal clustering inflates degrees of freedom
Wait time prediction target is endogenous to deployment use; operational feedback effects are not acknowledged
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting (imputation from test-period medians, StandardScaler on combined data) is data leakage despite correct temporal split
TCN receives 12-timestep sequences while linear regression receives a scalar aggregate; performance difference reflects feature richness, not architectural capability
Excluding patients without prior 12-month visits creates case mix bias; model will not generalize to first-time ED presentations
Paired t-test on autocorrelated ED encounter errors violates i.i.d. assumption; temporal clustering inflates degrees of freedom
Wait time prediction target is endogenous to deployment use; operational feedback effects are not acknowledged
